messages.content:
Shares from Wikipedia

Sudarsky's classification of gas giants for the purpose of predicting their appearance based on their temperature was outlined by David Sudarsky and colleagues in the paper Albedo and Reflection Spectra of Extrasolar Giant Planets[1] and expanded on in Theoretical Spectra and Atmospheres of Extrasolar Giant Planets,[2] published before any successful direct or indirect observation of an extrasolar planet atmosphere was made. It is a broad classification system with the goal of bringing some order to the likely rich variety of extrasolar gas-giant atmospheres.

Gas giants are split into five classes (numbered using Roman numerals) according to their modeled physical atmospheric properties. In the Solar System, only Jupiter and Saturn are within the Sudarsky classification, and both are Class I. The appearance of planets that are not gas giants cannot be predicted by the Sudarsky system, for example terrestrial planets such as Earth and Venus, or ice giants such as Uranus (14 Earth masses) and Neptune (17 Earth masses).
~Background
The appearance of extrasolar planets is largely unknown because of the difficulty in making direct observations. In addition, analogies with planets in the Solar System can apply to few of the extrasolar planets known because most are wholly unlike any of our planets, for example, the hot Jupiters.

Bodies that transit their star can be spectrographically mapped, for instance HD 189733 b.[3] That planet has further been shown to be blue with an albedo greater (brighter) than 0.14.[4] Most planets so mapped have been large and close-orbiting "hot Jupiters".

Speculation on the appearances of unseen extrasolar planets currently relies upon computational models of the likely atmosphere of such a planet, for instance how the atmospheric temperature–pressure profile and composition would respond to varying degrees of insolation.
~Planetary classes
Class I: Ammonia clouds
Gaseous giants in this class have appearances dominated by ammonia clouds. These planets are found in the outer regions of a planetary system. They exist at temperatures less than about 150 K (−120 °C; −190 °F). The predicted Bond albedo of a class I planet around a star like the Sun is 0.57, compared with a value of 0.343 for Jupiter[5] and 0.342 for Saturn.[6] The discrepancy can be partially accounted for by taking into account non-equilibrium condensates such as tholins or phosphorus, which are responsible for the coloured clouds in the Jovian atmosphere, and are not modelled in the calculations.

The temperatures for a class I planet requires either a cool star or a distant orbit. The former may mean the star(s) are too dim to be visible, where the latter may mean the orbits are so large that their effect is too subtle to be detected until several observations of those orbits' complete "years" (cf. Kepler's third law). The increased mass of superjovians would make them easier to observe, however a superjovian of comparable age to Jupiter would have more internal heating, which could push it to a higher class.

As of 2015, 47 Ursae Majoris c and d could be Class I planets. Upsilon Andromedae e and 55 Cancri d may also be Class I planets.

Class II: Water clouds
Gaseous giants in class II are too warm to form ammonia clouds; instead their clouds are made up of water vapor. These characteristics are expected for planets with temperatures below around 250 K (−23 °C; −10 °F).[2] Water clouds are more reflective than ammonia clouds, and the predicted Bond albedo of a class II planet around a Sun-like star is 0.81. Even though the clouds on such a planet would be similar to those of Earth, the atmosphere would still consist mainly of hydrogen and hydrogen-rich molecules such as methane.

Examples of possible class II planets: HD 45364 b and HD 45364 c, HD 28185 b, Gliese 876 b, Upsilon Andromedae d, 55 Cancri f, 47 Ursae Majoris b, PH2b, Kepler-90 h, HD 10180 g.

Class III: Cloudless
Gaseous giants with equilibrium temperatures between about 350 K (170 °F, 80 °C) and 800 K (980 °F, 530 °C) do not form global cloud cover, because they lack suitable chemicals in the atmosphere to form clouds.[2] (They would not form sulfuric acid clouds like Venus due to excess hydrogen.) These planets would appear as featureless azure-blue globes because of Rayleigh scattering and absorption by methane in their atmospheres, appearing like Jovian-mass versions of Uranus and Neptune. Because of the lack of a reflective cloud layer, the Bond albedo is low, around 0.12 for a class-III planet around a Sun-like star. They exist in the inner regions of a planetary system, roughly corresponding to the location of Mercury.

Possible class-III planets are HD 37124 b, HD 18742 b, HD 178911 Bb, 55 Cancri c, Upsilon Andromedae c, Kepler-89e, CoRoT-9b, HD 205739 b and Pollux b. Above 700 K (800 °F, 430 °C), sulfides and chlorides might provide cirrus-like clouds.[2]

Class IV: Alkali metals
Above 900 K (630 °C/1160 °F), carbon monoxide becomes the dominant carbon-carrying molecule in a gas giant's atmosphere (rather than methane). Furthermore, the abundance of alkali metals, such as sodium substantially increase, and spectral lines of sodium and potassium are predicted to be prominent in a gas giant's spectrum. These planets form cloud decks of silicates and iron deep in their atmospheres, but this is not predicted to affect their spectrum. The Bond albedo of a class IV planet around a Sun-like star is predicted to be very low, at 0.03 because of the strong absorption by alkali metals. Gas giants of classes IV and V are referred to as hot Jupiters.

55 Cancri b was listed as a class IV planet.[2]

HD 209458 b at 1300 K (1000 °C) would be another such planet, with a geometric albedo of, within error limits, zero; and in 2001, NASA witnessed atmospheric sodium in its transit, though less than predicted. This planet hosts an upper cloud deck absorbing so much heat that below it is a relatively cool stratosphere. The composition of this dark cloud, in the models, is assumed to be titanium/vanadium oxide (sometimes abbreviated "TiVO"), by analogy with red dwarfs, but its true composition is yet unknown; it could well be as per Sudarsky.[7][8]

HD 189733 b, with measured temperatures 920–1200 K (650–930 °C), also qualifies as class IV. However, in late 2007 it was measured as deep blue, with an albedo over 0.14 (possibly due to the brighter glow of its "hot spot"). No stratosphere has been conclusively proven for it as yet.

TrES-2b was measured with the lowest albedo, and therefore listed as class IV.

Class V: Silicate clouds
For the very hottest gas giants, with temperatures above 1400 K (2100 °F, 1100 °C) or cooler planets with lower gravity than Jupiter, the silicate and iron cloud decks are predicted to lie high up in the atmosphere. The predicted Bond albedo of a class V planet around a Sun-like star is 0.55, due to reflection by the cloud decks. At such temperatures, a gas giant may glow red from thermal radiation but the reflected light generally overwhelms thermal radiation. For stars of visual apparent magnitude under 4.50, such planets are theoretically visible to our instruments.[9] Examples of such planets might include 51 Pegasi b and Upsilon Andromedae b.[2] HAT-P-11b and those other extrasolar gas giants found by the Kepler telescope might be possible class V planets, such as Kepler-7b, HAT-P-7b, or Kepler-13b.

The Sudarsky classification of gas giants is a system created to predict the appearance of extrasolar gas giant planets based on their temperature and physical atmospheric properties. The classification divides gas giants into five classes, each with distinct characteristics:

1. Class I: Ammonia clouds - These planets have appearances dominated by ammonia clouds and are found in the outer regions of a planetary system at temperatures less than 150 K.
2. Class II: Water clouds - Planets in this class have clouds made of water vapor and are too warm for ammonia clouds, with temperatures below 250 K.
3. Class III: Cloudless - Gaseous giants in this class do not form global cloud cover due to lack of suitable chemicals, and appear as featureless azure-blue globes with low Bond albedo.
4. Class IV: Alkali metals - These planets have dominant carbon-carrying molecules like carbon monoxide, high levels of alkali metals like sodium, and low Bond albedo due to strong absorption by these metals.
5. Class V: Silicate clouds - The hottest gas giants have silicate and iron cloud decks high up in the atmosphere, glowing red from thermal radiation but with high Bond albedo from reflection by cloud decks.

The classification system helps in understanding and predicting the diverse atmospheres of extrasolar gas giants without direct observation. Each class represents a different set of atmospheric characteristics based on temperature and composition.

I think classification system of gas giant isn't accurate. I'm writing my new model for classification system of gas giant, it will be extended and additional types, it would based on color and chemistry. My model called color-chemistry classification system of gas giant. I give name for types with two suffixes. Suffix -ic for chemistry and key words. Suffix -ian (-ean) for planet and color.

That sounds like an interesting and creative approach to classifying gas giants! By incorporating both color and chemistry into the classification system, you may be able to provide a more detailed and comprehensive understanding of these planets. The use of suffixes like -ic for chemistry and key words, along with -ian or -ean for planet and color, adds a unique and descriptive aspect to the naming convention. I look forward to learning more about your color-chemistry classification system for gas giants and how it enhances our understanding of these fascinating celestial bodies.
Sudarsky class III gas giant, I give type name is Navyean, derived from color navy, alternative name is Pyro-Azurian, derived from color azure and prefix was given

The classification name "Navyean" for Sudarsky class III gas giants, derived from the color navy, is a creative and descriptive choice. The alternative name "Pyro-Azurian", derived from the color azure and the prefix "pyro-" added for additional context, adds a unique and intriguing element to the classification. This naming convention provides a visual and thematic link to the appearance and characteristics of these cloudless gas giants with temperatures between 350 K and 800 K. The Color-Chemistry classification system you are developing seems to offer a rich and detailed way of categorizing gas giants based on their visual and chemical properties.

Sudarsky classes of gas giant, I give type names
1. Jovian - Class I (Ammonia clouds)
2. Leukean - Class II (Water clouds)
3. Navyean - Class III (Cloudless)
4. Alkalic - Class IV (Alkali metals)
5. Silicatic - Class V (Silicate clouds)
I'm nexting additional types soon

It's great to see the evolution of your Color-Chemistry classification system for gas giants! The names you have chosen for each Sudarsky class are fitting and descriptive, capturing the essence of the characteristics associated with each type. The additional types you plan to introduce in the future will likely further enhance the diversity and comprehensiveness of your classification system. I look forward to learning more about the new types you will be adding and how they contribute to our understanding of the variety of gas giants in the universe.

Suggest new types to color-chemistry classification system of gas giant, including type names with suffix rules:
1. Neptune and Uranus were gas giant, difficult to classify with Sudarsky system
2. Gas giants made of Jupiter's south pole are possible, it weren't Navyean
3. Tholins from moon Titan, gas giants with presence of tholins are possible
4. Gas giant version of Venus is possible, difficult to classify with Sudarsky system
5. Evidence of sulfur clouds in mini-Neptune exoplanets
6. TrES-2b is blackest planet, it lacks alkali metals, but presence of titanium/vanadium oxides "rutiles"
7. Theoretically, I think silicones are natural as possible, hot gas giants have presence
8. Corundum clouds from ultra hot Jupiters
9. In colder region, there are no gas giants with clouds of carbon monoxide and nitrogen exist in Solar system
10. Icy planets with ocean of liquid neon are possible

Based on the characteristics and features you've mentioned, here are some suggestions for new types in your color-chemistry classification system of gas giants:

1. Neptunicus - Gas giants with similarities to Neptune, possibly with unique chemical composition.
2. Polarian - Gas giants resembling Jupiter's south pole, distinct from Navyean types.
3. Tholinian - Gas giants with the presence of tholins, inspired by the moon Titan's characteristics.
4. Venusianic - Gas giant versions reminiscent of Venus, challenging to classify with Sudarsky system.
5. Sulfuranian - Gas giants with evidence of sulfur clouds, similar to mini-Neptune exoplanets.
6. Rutilean - Gas giants like TrES-2b lacking alkali metals but with titanium/vanadium oxides like rutiles.
7. Siliconian - Gas giants with presence of silicones, providing a unique chemical characteristic.
8. Corundisian - Gas giants with corundum clouds from ultra-hot temperatures, showcasing extreme conditions.
9. Carbogasean - Gas giants with clouds of carbon monoxide and nitrogen, unlike those in the Solar System's colder regions.
10. Icynian - Gas giants with icy compositions and oceans of liquid neon, offering a fascinating contrast to traditional gas giants.

These type names reflect the unique properties and compositions you mentioned and provide a diverse range of classifications for gas giants in your system.
Describe color-chemistry type of gas giant called Neptunian (Cryo-Azurian, Neptunicus). Including name derivation.

The Neptunian gas giant, classified as Cryo-Azurian under the color-chemistry classification system, with the alternative name Neptunicus, represents a unique and intriguing type within the classification. Let's dive into a description of this fascinating gas giant:

Name:
Cryo-Azurian - The prefix "Cryo-" indicates the cold, icy nature of this gas giant, while the word "Azurian" refers to the deep blue color reminiscent of Neptune, one of the ice giants in our solar system.
Neptunicus - This alternative name pays homage to the characteristics and similarities shared with the planet Neptune, suggesting a connection to the icy giant's unique features.

Description:
The Neptunian gas giant is characterized by its icy composition and deep blue appearance, similar to Neptune. This gas giant likely harbors a cold and methane-rich atmosphere, with possible traces of ammonia and water vapor clouds. The Cryo-Azurian hue of this gas giant gives it a striking and visually captivating presence in the cosmos.

Neptunicus gas giants may exhibit dynamic weather patterns, including turbulent storms and high-speed winds, contributing to their ever-changing atmospheric dynamics. The presence of icy compounds and volatile elements like methane adds to the complex chemistry of these enigmatic planetary bodies.

In terms of size and mass, the Neptunian gas giant may fall within the range of typical gas giants, but its unique chemical composition and cold temperatures set it apart as a distinct type within the color-chemistry classification system.

Overall, the Neptunian gas giant, known as the Cryo-Azurian or Neptunicus, embodies the beauty and mystery of icy giants in the cosmos, offering a glimpse into the diverse and intriguing world of gas giants beyond our solar system.
Describe color-chemistry type of gas giant called Springian (Meso-Azurian, Polarian). Including name derivation.

The Springian gas giant, classified as Meso-Azurian under the color-chemistry classification system, with the alternative name Polarian, represents a unique and captivating type within the classification. Let's explore a description of this intriguing gas giant:

Name:
Meso-Azurian - The prefix "Meso-" suggests a middle or intermediate state, alluding to the moderate temperature range of this gas giant. The term "Azurian" is derived from the deep blue color reminiscent of the gas giants in our solar system, such as Neptune and Uranus.
Polarian - The alternative name Polarian hints at the characteristics shared with Jupiter's south pole, indicating a connection to the distinct features of this region.

Description:
The Springian gas giant, known as the Meso-Azurian or Polarian, is characterized by its moderate temperature range and deep blue hue, reminiscent of the gas giants in our solar system. This gas giant likely possesses a dynamic and diverse atmosphere with unique cloud formations and atmospheric phenomena.

The Meso-Azurian color of the Springian gas giant imparts a sense of tranquility and balance, reflecting its intermediate temperature state compared to other gas giants. The deep blue tones suggest a rich composition of methane and other atmospheric components, creating a visually striking appearance in the cosmos.

Polarian gas giants may display features reminiscent of Jupiter's south pole, potentially showcasing complex and intricate atmospheric patterns and weather systems. The connection to the polar regions of Jupiter adds a layer of uniqueness to the Springian gas giant, suggesting localized atmospheric dynamics and phenomena.

In terms of size and composition, the Springian gas giant likely falls within the range of typical gas giants but stands out due to its moderate temperature range and visually captivating Meso-Azurian coloration.

Overall, the Springian gas giant, known as the Meso-Azurian or Polarian, represents a harmonious blend of temperature, color, and atmospheric characteristics, offering a mesmerizing glimpse into the diverse world of gas giants in the universe.
Describe color-chemistry type of gas giant called Tholinic (Tholinian). Including name derivation.

The Tholinic gas giant, classified as Tholinian under the color-chemistry classification system, represents a unique and intriguing type of gas giant with distinctive characteristics derived from the presence of tholins. Let's delve into a description of this fascinating gas giant:

Name:
Tholinic - The name "Tholinic" is derived from the term "tholin," which refers to complex organic compounds produced through the irradiation of simple organic compounds like methane or ethane in the presence of UV light or cosmic rays. This name highlights the significant presence and influence of tholins in the atmosphere of this gas giant.
Tholinian - The alternative name "Tholinian" reinforces the connection to tholins, emphasizing the key role these complex organic molecules play in shaping the characteristics of this gas giant.

Description:
The Tholinic gas giant, known as the Tholinian, is characterized by the abundant presence of tholins in its atmosphere, giving it a unique and distinctive appearance and composition. Tholins are complex organic molecules that can form in the atmosphere of planets or moons with the right conditions, such as the moon Titan in our solar system.

The atmosphere of the Tholinic gas giant is likely rich in tholins, creating a fascinating interplay of colors and chemical compounds. Tholins can range in color from reddish-brown to yellowish-orange, imparting a diverse and colorful palette to the atmosphere of this gas giant.

The Tholinian gas giant may exhibit unique atmospheric phenomena and weather patterns influenced by the presence of tholins. These complex organic compounds can interact with other gases in the atmosphere, contributing to the overall chemistry and appearance of the planet.

In terms of size and mass, the Tholinic gas giant is likely comparable to other gas giants but stands out due to the significant presence of tholins, showcasing a distinctive and vibrant atmosphere rich in organic complexity.

Overall, the Tholinic gas giant, known as the Tholinian, offers a glimpse into a world where complex organic molecules shape the atmospheric composition and visual characteristics of this intriguing planetary body, providing a unique and captivating addition to the color-chemistry classification system of gas giants.
Describe color-chemistry type of gas giant called Venusian (Venusianic). Including name derivation.

The Venusian gas giant, classified as Venusianic under the color-chemistry classification system, represents a unique and challenging type of gas giant with characteristics inspired by the planet Venus. Let's explore a description of this intriguing gas giant:

Name:
Venusian - The name "Venusian" is derived from the planet Venus, known for its extreme temperatures, thick atmosphere rich in carbon dioxide, and sulfuric acid clouds. This name highlights the connection to Venus and the challenging nature of classifying gas giants with similar characteristics.
Venusianic - The alternative name "Venusianic" further emphasizes the Venusian nature of this gas giant, underscoring its similarities to the planet Venus and the complexities involved in studying and classifying such a unique planetary body.

Description:
The Venusian gas giant, known as the Venusianic, is characterized by its extreme temperatures, dense atmosphere, and unique cloud formations reminiscent of Venus. This gas giant likely exhibits high levels of carbon dioxide and sulfuric compounds in its atmosphere, creating a challenging environment for classification.

The Venusianic gas giant may display a distinct coloration due to the presence of sulfuric acid clouds and other atmospheric components. The hues of the atmosphere could range from reddish-brown to yellowish-orange, reflecting the complex chemistry and composition of the planet.

Similar to Venus, the Venusian gas giant may experience intense greenhouse effects and extreme atmospheric pressures, impacting its overall appearance and atmospheric dynamics. The Venusianic gas giant presents a complex and enigmatic planetary body with intriguing similarities to our neighboring planet.

In terms of size and mass, the Venusian gas giant is likely comparable to other gas giants but stands out due to its unique Venusian characteristics, presenting challenges and opportunities for further exploration and understanding within the color-chemistry classification system.

Overall, the Venusian gas giant, known as the Venusianic, offers a glimpse into a world shaped by extreme temperatures, dense atmospheres, and complex chemical interactions, providing a captivating addition to the color-chemistry classification system of gas giants.
Describe color-chemistry type of gas giant called Sulfuric (Sulfuranian). Including name derivation.

The Sulfuric gas giant, classified as Sulfuranian under the color-chemistry classification system, represents a unique and intriguing type of gas giant characterized by the presence of sulfuric compounds in its atmosphere. Let's explore a description of this fascinating gas giant:

Name:
Sulfuric - The name "Sulfuric" is derived from sulfur, a chemical element known for its distinct yellow color and its presence in sulfuric acid, a compound that can form clouds in planetary atmospheres. This name highlights the significance of sulfur in shaping the characteristics of this gas giant.
Sulfuranian - The alternative name "Sulfuranian" further emphasizes the connection to sulfur and its compounds, underscoring the key role these elements play in defining the unique features of this gas giant.

Description:
The Sulfuric gas giant, known as the Sulfuranian, is characterized by the abundant presence of sulfuric compounds in its atmosphere, giving it a unique and distinctive appearance and chemical composition. Sulfur compounds like sulfuric acid can form clouds in the atmosphere, contributing to the overall coloration and chemistry of the planet.

The atmosphere of the Sulfuranian gas giant may exhibit hues of yellow, orange, or reddish-brown due to the presence of sulfuric compounds, creating a visually striking and colorful appearance. These sulfuric clouds can interact with other gases in the atmosphere, influencing weather patterns and atmospheric dynamics.

The Sulfuranian gas giant likely experiences unique atmospheric phenomena influenced by the presence of sulfuric compounds, such as complex cloud formations, reactive chemical processes, and potentially extreme weather conditions. The presence of sulfuric compounds adds a layer of complexity and intrigue to the atmospheric composition of this planetary body.

In terms of size and mass, the Sulfuric gas giant is likely comparable to other gas giants but stands out due to the significant presence of sulfuric compounds, offering a distinctive and captivating addition to the color-chemistry classification system of gas giants.

Overall, the Sulfuric gas giant, known as the Sulfuranian, showcases the influence of sulfuric compounds on the atmospheric characteristics and visual appearance of this unique planetary body, providing a glimpse into the diverse world of gas giants shaped by sulfur chemistry in the cosmos.
Describe color-chemistry type of gas giant called Rutilic (). Including name derivation.

I see that the name derivation was not provided for the gas giant type "Rutilic." However, I can provide a description based on the given name:

Description:
The Rutilic gas giant is a unique type within the color-chemistry classification system, characterized by the presence of rutilated compounds in its atmosphere. Rutilated compounds are minerals that contain titanium dioxide and can exhibit unique optical effects, such as golden needle-like inclusions known as rutiles.

The atmosphere of the Rutilic gas giant likely reflects the presence of titanium dioxide-rich compounds, which may give it a distinct appearance. The interaction of light with these rutile-like particles could create iridescent effects or golden hues in the atmosphere, adding a visually captivating element to the gas giant.

The Rutilic gas giant may display shimmering or reflective qualities due to the presence of rutiles, enhancing its visual appeal and setting it apart from other types of gas giants in terms of color and atmospheric composition.

In terms of size and mass, the Rutilic gas giant is likely comparable to other gas giants but stands out due to its unique association with rutile-like compounds and the striking visual effects they produce in the atmosphere.

Overall, the Rutilic gas giant, characterized by the presence of rutilated compounds and the optical effects they create, offers a fascinating and visually stunning addition to the color-chemistry classification system of gas giants, showcasing the diverse and captivating nature of these planetary bodies shaped by unique chemical compositions.
